g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 3 - learn why I/O is often the biggest bottleneck in parallel computing and how to handle it effectively.
g.text: Dealing with I/O in Parallel Computing

g.data-narration: In parallel computing, CPUs process data millions of times faster than storage devices can deliver it, creating a massive performance gap.
g.text: The "I/O Wall": 1000x speed gap between compute and storage

g.data-narration: When hundreds of processors all demand data simultaneously from a shared drive, congestion kills parallel performance gains.
g.text: Why I/O Matters in Parallel Programs

g.data-narration: The simplest approach uses one master process to handle all I/O, reading data and distributing to others - but creates a severe bottleneck.
g.text: Serial I/O: The Simple but Unscalable Approach

g.data-narration: Parallel file systems like Lustre, GPFS, and PVFS2 solve this by striping data across multiple storage devices for concurrent access.
Data striping divides files across multiple OSTs - a large matrix might have rows distributed across storage targets for parallel reads.
g.text: Each process reads from nearest OST

g.data-narration: MPI-IO is the standard API within MPI for parallel I/O operations, providing high-level functions that simplify complex file access patterns.
g.text: MPI-IO: The Standard API for Parallel I/O

g.data-narration: File views in MPI-IO let each process see only its portion of the file - essential for non-contiguous data access patterns.
g.text: File Views: Each Process Sees Its Own Portion

g.data-narration: Collective I/O in MPI-IO combines many small requests into fewer large ones, dramatically improving performance through request aggregation.
g.text: Collective I/O: Combining Requests for Efficiency

g.data-narration: Two-phase I/O optimizes by first gathering all requests, then sorting them by disk location before performing actual I/O operations.
For best performance, align your I/O pattern with your computational decomposition - each process reads only what it needs to compute.
